name: Okumidori
tagline: First harvest, single cultivar. The matcha of quiet rooms.
description: Stone-milled from shade-grown Okumidori leaves picked in the first days of May. A clear, lingering umami with a soft floral finish — the standard against which other ceremonial matcha is measured.
story: Cultivated on a half-hectare plot east of the Uji river by the Tsuji family, fifth generation. The fields are tented with reed shades — honzu — for twenty-one days before harvest, a method largely unchanged since the sixteenth century.

brewing.method: Sift, add water, whisk in a brisk W-shape until a fine foam forms. Drink within minutes.

imageAlt: A bowl of bright green matcha tea with a bamboo whisk

name: Samidori
tagline: A bright, almost crystalline ceremonial grade from the Yahagi plain.
description: Nishio's mineral-rich soil and morning fog produce a matcha with unusual clarity. Samidori is the most demanding cultivar to grow — slow, low-yield — and rewards the patience with a clean, almost glass-like sweetness.
story: Grown by a co-op of seven families along the Yahagi river. The Nishio region produces only a fraction of Japan's matcha, but its tea is poured at imperial ceremonies and Zen temples across Honshu.

brewing.method: Whisk vigorously until foam is fine and uniform. Serve in a warmed chawan.

imageAlt: Matcha powder in a wooden scoop

name: Asahi
tagline: The cultivar of imperial preference. Vanishingly small harvest.
description: Asahi is the cultivar reserved for koicha — the thick matcha served at the most formal tea gatherings. Deep umami, an almost savory sweetness, and a long finish that holds for minutes.
story: Cultivated on a single hillside in Ogura by Mr. Hori, who keeps fewer than a hundred Asahi bushes. We receive thirty tins per year, no more.

brewing.method: Kneaded slowly with the whisk, not whipped. The result is a thick, syrupy tea, drunk in three and a half sips.

imageAlt: A traditional ceramic matcha bowl beside a bamboo whisk

name: Yabukita
tagline: An everyday matcha that takes nothing for granted.
description: The most-planted cultivar in Japan, here grown to a higher standard than its reputation suggests. A warm, full-bodied cup for daily practice — strong enough for usucha, gentle enough for morning.
story: Sourced from a cooperative of small growers in the Wazuka hills. The Yabukita cultivar dominates Japanese tea because it grew well, not because it grew best — but careful sourcing finds plots where it shines.

brewing.method: Whisk briskly until foam appears. A good cup for morning.

imageAlt: Whisked matcha in a dark stoneware bowl

name: Gokou
tagline: Dark, sweet, deeply aromatic. The connoisseur's daily cup.
description: Gokou is shaded longer than most cultivars, producing dark leaves that mill into an unusually rich matcha. Sweetness leans toward dark chocolate; aroma fills the room before the bowl reaches your lips.
story: Grown under thirty days of shade — five days longer than ceremonial standard — by a single farm cooperative south of Nishio city.

brewing.method: Whisk into a thick foam. Pairs unusually well with a piece of dark chocolate.

name: Saemidori
tagline: Volcanic soil, southern sun. A bright, modern matcha.
description: Chiran sits at the foot of a volcano on Japan's southern island, where warmer days produce a brighter, more vivid green. The result is a matcha with snap and lift — less weight, more clarity.
story: A young farm run by Naomi Tanaka, who returned to her family's fields after twelve years in Tokyo. We were her first export buyer.

brewing.method: Whisk to a brisk foam. Serves cold beautifully — try over ice with a splash of milk.

imageAlt: A glass of iced matcha latte

name: Uji Culinary
tagline: Late-harvest, robust. Built for baking, lattes, and recipe work.
description: A late-harvest Uji blend, sun-grown for body and color. Not for drinking straight — but for matcha financiers, ice cream, and lattes, the depth is unmatched by anything we've found at the price.
story: Blended from second-flush leaves across the Uji region. The same growers we work with for ceremonial grades — culinary is what their kitchens use, and now ours.

brewing.method: Steam milk and whisk in. Holds its color and bitterness through 180°C oven heat.

imageAlt: Matcha powder on a wooden surface beside ingredients

name: The Starter Set
tagline: Everything you need, considered. A whisk, a bowl, a tin.
description: Our Okumidori ceremonial matcha, a hand-cut bamboo chasen from a Takayama whiskmaker, a wood-fired chawan from a small Tamba kiln, and a bamboo scoop. Packed in furoshiki cloth.
story: We assembled this for people who wanted to start, and were tired of being upsold. Every piece is one we use ourselves.

brewing.method: Comes with a printed brewing card and our preferred technique.

imageAlt: Tea ceremony tools arranged on a wooden surface
